title: Learning module

Specifically, designed address student learning outcomes: basic understanding structure mathematics travel demand model, Understand types scenarios travel demand models appropriate , Understand limitations uncertainty travel demand modeling, able constructive conversations travel modelers. implemented R package (R Core Team 2026), several advantages. Using First Four-Step Model package assignment gives students gentle introduction language potentially piques interest learning . package several key design goals: four steps model map directly onto four functions package; place tradeoff simplicity predictive accuracy, simplicity chosen; can estimated location United States using publicly-available data; intuitive tools visualize model inputs, outputs, parameters, students can interpret understand model; Preparing land-use transportation scenarios simple; runs computer student likely (including Windows, Macs, Chromebooks); depends R common R packages easily installed Comprehensive R Archive Network (CRAN). meet goals, model highly simplified, certainly affects predictive accuracy, predictive accuracy one goals. title: Methods and input data
text: Trip generation based National Household Travel Survey. model uses four time periods: overnight (7:00 pm–5:59 ), Peak (6:00 –9:59 ), midday (10:00 –3:59 pm), PM Peak (4:00 pm–6:59 pm). model divides trips three purposes: home-based work (HBW), home-based (HBO), non-home-based (NHB). fewer trip types time periods might included production travel models, consistent general practice dividing trips time day type. Household-level trip counts estimated time period trip type using simple linear regression trip count dependent variable independent variables number vehicles, household size, household income, Census tract residential density, number workers. results 12 regression equations, time period trip purpose. Household income represented dummy variables less $35,000, $35,000–$74,999, $75,000–$99,999, $100,000 . regression models disaggregate, household-level models, whereas four-step model aggregate model using marginal data TAZ level (simplicity, TAZs correspond directly Census tracts). Specifically, model uses household size (topcoded 4), number workers (topcoded 3), number vehicles (topcoded 3), income (categories used regression). apply household-level model aggregate data, disaggregate data household-level records (.e. create synthetic population) using iterative proportional fitting seed matrix derived Integrated Public Use Microdata Sample 2021 Five-Year American Community Survey data entire US (Ruggles et al. 2024). seed matrix ships software. regressions predict household-level tripmaking, re-aggregated tract level. NHTS provide sufficient spatial detail estimate trip attractions. Instead, use Puget Sound Household Travel Survey, includes origin destination Census tract public-use dataset (Puget Sound Regional Council 2024). calculate number HBW HBO trips time period non-home end Census tract Puget Sound region. NHB trips, production attraction ends clearly defined. therefore assume NHB attractions productions half total number NHB trips originating destined tract. extrapolate data tracts outside region, build linear regression models trip type time period based total employment employment retail, education, accomodation/food services US Census Bureau Longitudinal Employer-Household Dynamics Origin-Destination Employment Statistics. balance total attractions trip type time period match estimated productions.

(2024). intrazonal trips, assume travel distance 0.52s0.52 \sqrt{s}, ss area TAZ. based Monte Carlo simulation average distance random points square. two opposing factors bias . title: Methods and input data
text: network assignment uses Frank-Wolfe traffic assignment algorithm (Boyles, Lownes, Unnikrishnan 2023). First, convert production-attraction format home-based trips origin-destination format, using directionality factors estimated NHTS. calculate peak hourly vehicle flows period using average vehicle occupancy period trip type, assumed “peaking factor” accounts proportion traffic time period occurs busiest hour—example, assume 45% traffic three-hour PM Peak period occurs busiest hour. , run assignment algorithm, impedances based Bureau Public Roads-style function: tcongested=(1+0.6[fc]5)tfreeflow t_{\mathrm{congested}} = \left(1 + 0.6 \left[\frac{f}{c}\right] ^ 5 \right) t_{\mathrm{freeflow}} ff predicted flow, cc link capacity, tcongestedt_{\mathrm{congested}} tfreeflowt_\mathrm{freeflow} congested free-flow link travel times. factors 0.6 5 Southern California Association Governments travel demand model (Southern California Association Governments 2012). derive network OpenStreetMap. default, retain major roads—motorways, trunk, primary roads (associated ramps). Furthermore, run assignment algorithm “relative gap”—measure error estimate—1%, rather typically recommended 0.01% (Boyce, Ralevic-Dekic, Bar-Gera 2004), improve performance. work © 2026 Matt Bhagat-Conway licensed CC 4.0

title: Scenario creation
text: two ways create new land use scenarios. simpler method use add_households function. function modifies scenario adding households particular characteristics particular area. code adds 20,000 households Census tract 37037020801, location large new residential development. households add specified tabular format. case, four-person, two-worker households; half three cars income $150,000/year, half two cars income $75,000/year. Household size can take values 1 4; workers vehicles can take values 0 3, income can take values 0, 35000, 75000, 100000, representing low end income bin. Every model includes default “baseline” scenario represents current conditions American Community Survey; scenarios can built extending modifying baseline. want add households one tract, simply use add_households build scenario one piece time. control needed, scenarios can created tabular format hand using external tools. format shown @tbl-demographic-scenario, specifying number households different household size, income, vehicle ownership, number worker categories. user ensure number households tract consistent across marginal categories (.e. four hhsize categories total 1000 households, four income categories must also total 1000 households). Specification demographic scenario {#tbl-demographic-scenario} create scenario, first export baseline scenario Excel format: can modify scenario using external tools. addition tab specifying household counts, also tab specifying employment tract, used generate attractions. column names match LEHD LODES dataset. done modifying scenario, can load back R running: can replace projected name want (long valid R identifier, e.g. spaces). scenarios created model saved (see Estimation) available use model loaded.
code: model$scenarios$chatham_park = model$scenarios$baseline |>   add_households(     "37037020801",     tibble::tribble(       ~hhsize , ~workers , ~vehicles , ~income , ~n    ,             4 ,        2 ,         3 ,  150000 , 10000 ,             4 ,        2 ,         2 ,   75000 , 10000     )   ) save_landuse_scenario(model$scenarios$baseline, "scenario.xlsx") model$scenarios$projected = load_landuse_scenario("scenario.xlsx")

title: Scenario creation
text: Changes network just important changes land use. Currently, First Four-Step Model supports changes existing links. Two link attributes can changed: lane count roadway type. roadway type uses OpenStreetMap highway tag taxonomy; notable values ‘motorway’, ‘trunk’, ‘primary’. Changing attributes link requires determining OpenStreetMap ‘way ID’, can done either looking data openstreetmap.org, exporting network GIS format investigating GIS: Link attributes can modified using modify_links function. example, code widens US 15-501 north Pittsboro, NC, two three lanes direction, upgrades motorway. work © 2026 Matt Bhagat-Conway licensed CC 4.0

title: Highly Simplified Four-Step Travel Demand Models for Teaching
text: “First Four Step Model” simple four-step model can install easily run quickly consumer-grade computers. can estimated US metropolitan area using publicly-available data. transportation planning engineering programs teach travel demand modeling extent. experience, almost invariably taught ‘bottom-’ approach. Students take classes transportation planning, econometrics, choice modeling. Students work individual components demand models, trip generation mode choice. Often, students never “put together” run regional model start finish. vast majority learning time spent theory mathematics, rather applications. First Four Step Model facilitates ‘top-’ approach running complete model one first steps. use approach introductory Planning Methods course, spend week discussing transportation modeling engineering, conclusion students run simple demand model Research Triangle region North Carolina. Full details learning module available . Theory mathematics paramount build run travel demand models . small group students, however. metropolitan planning organizations DOT’s, demand models estimated run consultants small -house team. Consumers model output larger group: transportation planners engineers, land use planners, developers, advocates, . larger group, cursory understanding mathematics required; general mechanisms assumptions model relies far important. students transportation planning engineering programs fall latter group. better understanding modeling among group help promote better communications modelers model consumers. Consumers aware model can can’t , able come situations model may helpful. Understanding also promote “healthy skepticism” model, enabling feedback users model ultimately leading better models decision support. Students interested modeling may take classes, students first-hand experience demand models—something many students get today, even taking many classes modeling. First Four-Step Model never appropriate production travel demand modeling. also appropriate sole teaching tool students ultimately become modelers. However, useful first exercise even courses focus demand modeling, students can chance work simple model diving complex theories software necessary detailed education area.

what: Up through the mode choice step, everything is done with productions at home and attractions at work. For assignment, we need origins and destinations. what: This calibrates the trip distance beta, using the method described in Merlin (2020) A new method using medians to calibrate single-parameter spatial interaction models, JTLU. The basic idea is that we adjust the beta for the decay function until half the distance-weighted accessibility occurs in the median travel distance. For NHB, productions and attractions will be the same — calibrate_trip_distance_beta
title: This calibrates the trip distance beta, using the method described in Merlin (2020) A new method using medians to calibrate single-parameter spatial interaction models, JTLU. The basic idea is that we adjust the beta for the decay function until half the distance-weighted accessibility occurs in the median travel distance. For NHB, productions and attractions will be the same — calibrate_trip_distance_beta
text: calibrates trip distance beta, using method described Merlin (2020) new method using medians calibrate single-parameter spatial interaction models, JTLU. basic idea adjust beta decay function half distance-weighted accessibility occurs median travel distance. NHB, productions attractions

title: This calibrates the trip distance beta, using the method described in Merlin (2020) A new method using medians to calibrate single-parameter spatial interaction models, JTLU. For NHB, productions and attractions will be the same — calibrate_trip_distance_beta

what: Internal IPF code, similar to what was previously done in R. For efficiency, marginal names and values are converted to integers before calling. — do_ipf
title: Internal IPF code, similar to what was previously done in R. For efficiency, marginal names and values are converted to integers before calling. — do_ipf
text: Internal IPF code, similar previously done R. efficiency, marginal names values converted integers calling.

title: Internal IPF code, similar to what was previously done in R. For efficiency, marginal names and values are converted to integers before calling. — do_ipf
code: do_ipf(   orig_counts,   marginal_values,   target_marginals,   target_values,   target_counts,   convergence )

title: Internal IPF code, similar to what was previously done in R. For efficiency, marginal names and values are converted to integers before calling. — do_ipf
text: orig_counts numeric vector number household sampled seed matrix marginal_values integer matrix one row per household one column per marginal, indicating value household marginal. target_marginals along target_values target_counts indicate target values marginal . target_values value marginal (e.g. income, might 2 represent $25-50,000) target_counts number households expected value. convergence largest error absolute terms can tolerated, e.g. 0.01 -> errors 0.02 hhs marginal.

what: This estimates mode choice models based on the NHTS This is much simplified from the traditional mode choice models used in demand modeling, because we don't have attributes of the alternatives (or enough information to create them). So we just use demographics and mode-invariant trip characteristics to estimate mode choice. — estimate_mode_choice_model
title: This estimates mode choice models based on the NHTS This is much simplified from the traditional mode choice models used in demand modeling, because we don't have attributes of the alternatives (or enough information to create them). what: This function takes the output from igraph and updates the flows using it. Doing this in R requires too many levels of indirection and is really slow, so we hand this little piece off to Rust (though this does require a complation step so may ultimately not be worth it). — update_flows
